### File: outputs/AWS/AWS Speciality/advanced-networking-specialty/14_Direct Connect (DX)/119_Introduction to DX Virtual Interfaces (VIFs).txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: Hi. as of now, we have seen what is DX connections and how to create those connections.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And now let's focus on the next step.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: That is DX virtual interfaces.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: While DX connection provides the physical connectivity, the virtual interfaces provide the logical connectivity.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So in order to really have the traffic flowing end to end, you need to create the virtual interfaces and you have to make sure that the BGP session is established and then actually packet starts flowing through the DX connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So you must first provision the virtual interfaces on top of your DX connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now when you create the virtual interfaces, you have to use 802.1 Q VLAN tagging feature and this is what we had covered in the previous lecture.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And now we will see its applicability in this context.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: All these services have the public endpoints or public IPs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, one way to reach to these IPs is over the internet and this is how typically we access AWS services, but you can access these services over the DirectConnect, which is a private network between your data center and AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Second type of the VIF is a private VIF as the name suggests, it allows you to connect to the VPCs and then all the resources inside VPC which are typically your EC2 machines or ENIs can be accessed over the private IP.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: Virtual Private Gateway
- Key Insights: And with private VIF, either you can directly connect it to virtual private Gateway which is attached to the VPC or you can attach private VIF to DirectConnect Gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: And then same DX Gateway can be attached to multiple VPCs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And we will see all those architecture in the following lectures.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: Third type of the VIF is Transit VIF and this allows the connectivity to the Transit Gateway, but you can't directly attach a Transit VIF to the Transit Gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: For this, you have to also have the DX Gateway in between.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: And then you can attach Transit VIF to the Transit Gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: AWS Direct Connect, Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: And once you attach the DX connection to the Transit Gateway, it can be then attached to the hundreds and thousands of the VPCs.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And once that happens, now you create a logical connectivity on top of this connection in the form of VIF.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: Virtual Private Gateway
- Key Insights: So you create a private VIF and attach it to virtual private Gateway attached to the VPC.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And while creating the VIF, you assign the VLAN tag and these VLAN tags help segregate the traffic across multiple VIFs on the same connections.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And we already talked about how this VLAN tagging works.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So likewise, you can create another private VIF which goes to DX Gateway and then one DX Gateway can be attached to the multiple VPCs and for connecting to the public services, you create a public VIF.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, in all these cases, there are different via tagging that we are using so that traffic can be isolated.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: OK.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's look at the VIF with respect to the Dedicated or a Hosted connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So if you are using the Dedicated connection, then you can have multiple VIFs created on top of the same connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And as of today, the limit is 50 VIFs, but in the future, it may increase.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So always refer AWS documentation for the current limits.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: But in case of Hosted connection, which means the connection is owned by the DX partner and then it is allowing you to use that connection.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 32:

Line 34:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology

- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

Line 6:

Line 7:

- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

Line 10:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration

Line 11:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Routing Domain Design

Line 15:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 16:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, in that case, all the routes will automatically get into your subnets, and any traffic which falls under that IP address ranges, will be routed through the private VIF, if the traffic is going from AWS, to your side of the network.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 18:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, instead of having small prefix ranges, you can just advertise one large prefix range, which, ultimately, covers all those small address ranges.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And, note that the propagated routes will take precedence, over the default route, into your subnets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That means, if you have a route for the internet, 0.0.0.0, is the destination and the target is internet gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And, if there is, a match, happens, with your propagated route, for the target, which resides in the customer network site, then instead of internet gateway, it will choose the route through the private VIF, because it's a propagated route, which your subnet learnt, over the BGP session from the customer network site.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration

- Key Insights: So in this lecture let's understand another feature of AWS Direct Connect that is link aggregation groups or called as lags.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, as the name suggests, it aggregates multiple links.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect

Line 5:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 6:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So the basic reason for using the lags is to aggregate multiple DX connections so that if, say you have four connections with one GB s bandwidth, you can aggregate that into a single pipe of four GB s connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So using the lags you get increased bandwidth.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And also you get a failover failover in the sense that if there are four connections, even if one connection fails, then also your lags still operates.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you will get maybe three GB s bandwidth, but your connection will work so it consolidate all your connections into a single logical connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now for lag it uses a protocol called Link Aggregation Control Protocol Lacp.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 12:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 16:

Line 17:

Line 18:

- Services: (none explicit)

### File: outputs/AWS/AWS Speciality/advanced-networking-specialty/14_Direct Connect (DX)/135_DX Failure detection with BFD.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Hello and welcome to this lecture.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover, Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: Now that we have seen some of the architecture when it comes to the high availability of direct connect connections.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 3:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's understand how to failover fast, which means if you detect the failure in one of the link, how fast you can failover to another link.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And that is important because then ultimately it would reduce your network downtime or a network outage.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So for direct connect, there is something called BFD.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 6:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you enable the BFD that is bidirectional forwarding detection, then you can minimize this failover time to a secondary link.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So in the earlier lecture, if you remember, we already saw what PhD means in generic sense, and it helps you to take the failure in the network links and it detects that real quick so that you can fail over to the secondary link.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover, Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: And now in this lecture, let's understand how BFT is applied for the direct Connect connection and then how much failover time you would have if you enable the PhD and if you don't enable the BFT.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover, Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So while using the multiple connection or using VPN backup, it's always important to have fast failure to a redundant connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So if you don't apply the BFD, so by default BGP waits for three keeps alive to fail at a hold down time of 90 seconds, so it takes up to 90 seconds to detect the failure if BFT is not applied.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: However, as you know, with BFD, that time can be reduced to as low as one second.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, BFD is not something which is, you know, enabled at your end.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Automatically, however, will enable the BFD by default on DCS connections.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But as I said, you need to make sure that it is also enabled at your end.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if you talk about the calculation of one second typically sets the BFD liveliness detection interval to 300 milliseconds and then BFD liveliness detection count is three, which means if there are three consecutive failures, that means the peer does not send the response back for three consecutive packets in the interval of 300 milliseconds.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Overall, around in 900 milliseconds, you will detect that failure.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you can think around within one second.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And these settings would differ as per your router type.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 19:

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: AWS Direct Connect

Line 8:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 9:

- Services: AWS Direct Connect

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 10:

- Services: AWS Direct Connect

- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 12:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 13:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In case of hosted connection, it is...
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: A DirectConnect partner from whom you are taking the hosted connection will do this activity for you.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you are to make sure ports are correct, the routers are powered on, the Tx and Rx signals are being received.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now we know that using CloudWatch we can see that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then if there are no Tx/Rx signal, then you can contact the colocation provider and they can give you the report of the Tx and Rx signals.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, looking at that report, you can see that where is the problem with respect to the signals.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Also, you know, in the CloudWatch, it also provides the physical error count on the AWS devices.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So that will also give you some indication of the problem at the physical layer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And nothing out of this works, then obviously you can contact AWS support.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is how you will troubleshoot the Layer 1 issues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, if the connection is up, but still your VIFs are down, so you might've created a public VIF or a private VIF and those VIFs are not working, then you are to make sure the IP addresses that you provide while creating the VIFs, right, those are correct.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, if you remember, while creating the public VIF you provide the public IP ranges on the customer router end as well as AWS end for BGP session, as well as you also advertise the prefixes on the customer end.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now those IPs must be owned by the customer, you know that, so you are to make sure it is correct, VLAN IDs are correct.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So what are tags you are using now?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You know that if it's the same connection VLAN tags should be unique across all VIFs on the same connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you have to make sure VLAN IDs are correct.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The router MAC address in the ARP table, that means...
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You know, ultimately at Layer 2, the IP addresses are mapped to the MAC addresses for the routers, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So in that case, you are to make sure MAC addresses are correct.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If not, then you can also try cleaning your ARP tables and also you are to make sure if it's a issue with the VLAN, then VLAN truncing is enabled at the intermediate devices.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now this is more on your side of the network because the connection from DirectConnect location to AWS is, again, managed by AWS, but from DirectConnect location to the on-premise data center, whatever network is there, if there are multiple hubs and routers in between you are to make sure VLAN truncing is enabled there as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal; Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And of course, if nothing out of this works, then contact AWS support.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now assuming that your connection is up, the VIFs are up, but still the traffic is not going, and you see that the BGP session is down.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So for that, you are to make sure when you give the parameters for the BGP session, you give the correct ASN numbers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, you know that if you are using the public VIF and you are doing a public ASN, that ASN has to be owned by the customer and AWS verifies that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you also provide the peer IPs for the BGP session.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Those needs to be correct as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, in case of public VIF you know that these IP addresses should also be owned by the customer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then comes the MD5 Authentication key.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 42:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Make sure that there are no additional spaces and you provide your own MD5 Auth key or you use the one generated by AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 43:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But when you expose these configurations and apply the same key on your side of the router, that means customer side of the router, you don't make mistake while putting those keys.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 44:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you know that in case of private VIF you can't advertise, or you shouldn't advertise more than 100 prefixes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Constraint or limitation signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 45:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Otherwise the BGP session goes down and for public VIF this limit is 1000 prefixes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 46:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So no more than 1000 prefixes should be advertised from customer into AWS side.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 47:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You also know that for BGP session, it uses TCP 179 port number.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 48:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you are make sure that the firewalls at customer end are not blocking this port.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 49:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Otherwise you can always look at the BGP logs and again, if nothing works, your BGP session is still down, you can contact AWS support.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 50:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's take the final approach where everything is okay, connection is okay, VIFs are up, BGP session is also up, but still the traffic is not reaching to the destination.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 51:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So in that case, it could be a problem with your maybe routing tables, but we can see that if you are advertising the right routes for the on-premises prefixes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal; Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 52:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Because on AWS end those routes will be propagated, and if your IP address is not part of the prefixes that you advertise, then AWS won't route that traffic to your network using the DirectConnect connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 53:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now for public VIF it should be publicly routable prefixes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 54:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We already talked about that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 55:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now on AWS side, you are to make sure your security group, which is attached to EC2 machines or ENIs, are not blocking the traffic or at subnet level network access control lists are not blocking the traffic from the source IP addresses of your on-premise network.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 56:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And you should also check your VPC Route table that there is route exist to take that traffic from AWS to on-premise network.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal; Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 57:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now these routes could be static or these routes could be dynamic propagated one.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 58:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So make sure those routes are available, without that traffic cannot flow in either direction.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Routing-path and control-plane signal.

Line 59:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, if you want to further understand these troubleshooting steps, then I would recommend that you visit the AWS documentation where the troubleshooting steps have been explained in further details.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 60:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But as of now, that's it for troubleshooting AWS DirectConnect connection issues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

- Services: (none explicit)

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 58:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: This looks small, but if this VPC is doing, you know, terabytes and petabytes of data transfer or this transit gateway, then this is a huge cost and it would make sense to avoid using transit gateway in such scenarios.

Line 59:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect, Virtual Private Gateway
- Key Insights: So the choice for us is why don't we directly use a private Vif you can have your private Vif over this direct connect location and then you can connect it to the gateway or you can directly connect private Vif to the virtual private gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 60:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But there is no cost for the gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 61:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So I would recommend that you use the gateway instead, because in the future, if there are many such vpcs to be connected, then you can connect through the same gateway over the same private wave.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 62:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it would make sense that you directly connect that to this another gateway.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 63:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: That way you avoid the cost of this transit gateway.

Line 64:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Okay.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 65:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway
- Key Insights: And finally, if you want to connect to the public services, like, for example, S3, again, you don't want to send that data through Transit Gateway or through your VPC because there would be additional cost.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 66:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 67:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity

Line 68:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 69:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And in that case you avoid all those charges.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 70:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 71:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

Line 72:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 73:
- Concepts: Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 74:

- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

Line 2:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context

- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So that means that your whole infrastructure through your NAT gateway and then your internet gateway or just directly through your internet gateway does access DynamoDB.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But all this traffic goes through the public internet and you may have other services such as CloudWatch and Amazon S3 that you also wanna access without going through the internet.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: You wanna access them privately.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the idea is that you're going to use VPC endpoints so that your instances don't have to go through the public internet, they can just go directly through the private AWS network to these services.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's consider this architecture that we know already, so we have public subnets with a NAT gateway and it's EC2 instance, a private subnet, and then we're going to have an internet gateway so that we know we can access the Amazon SNS service for example, through different ways.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So if we look at the private subnet and the EC2 instance in it, to access the Amazon SNS service, the EC2 instance can go through the NAT gateway which then goes to the internet gateway and then accesses publicly the Amazon SNS service and this is the same for the EC2 instance in the public subnet, it goes directly through the internet gateway to the Amazon SNS service.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this option is fine but it costs just a lot of money because, well, we have to go through NAT gateway, which has a cost, and then we have to go through the internet gateway which doesn't have a cost but still there is obviously many hops and it's not very efficient.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: VPC Endpoint
- Key Insights: So instead, I'm just representing the same Amazon SNS service, but somewhere else on the diagram, we can use a VPC endpoint.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: VPC Endpoint
- Key Insights: And the idea is that the VPC endpoint is deployed within your VPC and by doing some networking, we can actually make the EC2 instance in the private subnet go through the VPC endpoint directly into the Amazon SNS service and the network has never left AWS, which is really good.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: AWS PrivateLink
- Key Insights: So, every AWS service is publicly exposed, it has public a URL, and if you use VPC endpoints, they are powered by something called AWS PrivateLink because you access these services privately.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then you can connect to any of these services by AWS using a private network instead of going over the public internet.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: High Availability and Failover
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: These VPC endpoints are redundant and they scale horizontally, and they're going to remove the need for an internet gateway or a NAT gateway to access AWS services, thus simplifying your network infrastructure by a lot.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 16:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is the general idea behind the VPC endpoints now, we have two types of VPC endpoints.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: AWS PrivateLink
- Key Insights: We have the Interface Endpoints and these are powered by PrivateLink and we have the Gateway Endpoints.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the Interface Endpoints provision an ENI, so it's a private IP address in your VPC and this ENI is an entry point to your private AWS service.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so for this, we must attach a security group because there is an ENI and it supports most AWS services if not all, and there's a cost to it, it's a cost per hour and it's a cost per gigabyte of data processed.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: AWS PrivateLink, VPC Endpoint
- Key Insights: So here we go, we have an EC2 instance and a private subnet and thanks to a PrivateLink VPC endpoint of type interface then we can access through the ENI to the service.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this works for every single service out there.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now we have the Gateway Endpoint and they're special.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Private connectivity and segmentation signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: They provision what's called a gateway and this has to be used as a target in a route table.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this does not leverage IP addresses, this is not a leverage security groups, it's just a target in a route table and you need to remember that.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity, Routing Domain Design
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And there are only two targets for a Gateway Endpoint, it is Amazon S3 and DynamoDB, but the advantage that this is free and this scales automatically because it's only a route table access.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 27:
- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: VPC Endpoint

- Concepts: Private East-West Connectivity
- Services: (none explicit)

### File: outputs/AWS/aws-certified-solutions-architect-associate-SAA-C03/27_Networking - VPC/341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about Direct Connect, and you may also see it as DX in the exam.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it provides a dedicated private connection from the remote network into your VPC.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So you need to set up that Direct Connect connection and it is using an AWS Direct Connect location.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: Virtual Private Gateway
- Key Insights: You also need to set up a virtual private gateway on your VPC side to make the connectivity between your on-premise data center and AWS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The idea is that on the same connection you can access both public resources such as Amazon history and private resources, such as EC2 Instances using the the public VIF and the private VIF.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: So what are the use cases for Direct Connect?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, you get increased bandwidth throughputs, that means that if you're working with large data sets, is going to be faster because it doesn't go over the public internet, and also you're going to have lower cost because you're using a private connection.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes connectivity operational detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: AWS Direct Connect
- Key Insights: Also, if you have connectivity issues using the public internet, we'll using Direct Connect you get a more consistent network experience, again because it is private.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Hybrid connectivity architecture signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Connectivity Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 24:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: Site-to-Site VPN

Line 25:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration, Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology
- Services: Transit Gateway

Line 26:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration, Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology

Line 27:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration
- Services: Site-to-Site VPN

Line 28:
- Concepts: Hybrid WAN Integration

Line 31:

## Step 2 â€” Consolidation

### 1. Concepts List
- Connectivity Operational Context
- High Availability and Failover
- Hybrid WAN Integration

- Routing Domain Design
- Segmentation and Isolation
- Transit and Hub-Spoke Topology

### 2. Services List
- AWS Client VPN
- AWS Cloud WAN
- AWS Direct Connect
- AWS PrivateLink
- Customer Gateway
- Direct Connect Gateway
- Site-to-Site VPN

- VPC Peering
- Virtual Private Gateway

- route propagation

### 4. Use Cases
- 053_Introduction to VPC private connectivity options.txt:3: But now let's expand our networking beyond the VPC, and let's see how you can connect VPCs to other networks, for example, to other VPCs or to the on premises network, or say within the AWS.
- 053_Introduction to VPC private connectivity options.txt:11: There are different types for example gateway endpoint interface endpoint and as well as interface endpoint extension to the private link.
- 053_Introduction to VPC private connectivity options.txt:17: And for that you would have to expose your application over the public IPS to the internet.
- 053_Introduction to VPC private connectivity options.txt:21: For example, you might have to encrypt the traffic at layer three or layer four, or at layer seven using Https, right.
- 053_Introduction to VPC private connectivity options.txt:30: So we will see what kind of architecture you can implement for this kind of use case.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:4: And that's the simplest option you can look at to start with but then you need to understand when it is appropriate to use VPC peering and when you would use another option.

- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:1: Okay, before we move to the summary, I thought let's talk about this topic quickly because many a times a lot of learners ask this question, as in when to use VPC peering and when to use Privatelink, or when to use a transit gateway.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:5: But it is always important to understand when to use what right.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:9: So VPC peering is useful when there are many resources that should communicate between the peered VPC.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:16: So you should use it when you want to allow access to only a single application hosted inside your VPC to other VPCs without peering that VPCs.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:21: Further, when there is overlapping Cidrs, you know that you cannot connect them to the transit gateways or VPC peering.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:23: Further, there are also some limitations when it comes to the VPC peering connection.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:24: Next VPC endpoint can be used to connect VPCs with overlapping sides, and that is one of the key benefit of it.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:25: Um, over using the VPC peering connection or the transit gateways and VPC endpoints can also be accessed from other networks, for example, peered VPCs or transit gateways, or a VPN connection or the Direct Connect.

- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:13: But just to give you a brief history, Transit Gateways were introduced in 2018, and intent was to simplify the AWS networking when it comes to managing the multiple VPCs, and when you have to have the hybrid connectivity between AWS and on-premises network.

- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:18: Now when you create a transit gateway, there will be a default route table of that transit gateway.

- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:45: Now this is how the routing happens when you use transit gateway.

- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:11: And when I say in each availability zone, it doesn't mean it's an ideal, but this is how it should be because transit gateway has a limitation where if you create the transit gateway attachment in a given AZ, then only the subnet from that AZ would be able to communicate with the transit gateway through that attachment.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:21: So when you attach VPC to the transit gateway, you must enable one or more AZs to be used by transit gateway so that the traffic can be routed to the VPC subnet in those particular AZs.

- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:16: In order to connect this appliances to the Transit Gateway before creating this GRE tunnel, there should be a transport mechanism.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:20: And this is a case when your appliances running inside a VPC, but if your appliances are running on-premises, then you can use Direct Connect as your transport attachment.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:46: So let's look at the first case when the VPC transport attachment is used.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:52: Now in order for your on-premise network to connect to this VPC, you can use different mechanisms.

- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:8: Now rest of the VPN features remains almost same with some differences, which we are going to talk about soon, but ultimately, you have now all your VPN terminating on the transit gateway, and to further scale this architecture, if you have multiple branch offices, then, of course, you will have multiple VPN connections terminating on the same transit gateway, so this is one of the very popular architecture when you want to implement the hybrid connectivity between AWS VPCs and your on-premises data center network.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:10: Now, one more additional feature that AWS VPN provides when you connect that to the transit gateway is accelerated VPN.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:15: There are 400-plus, but the way it optimizes your network traffic is that the traffic from on-premise network goes to the nearest edge location, and from there, it is already connected to AWS backbone network, so your path to the transit gateway mainly stays on the AWS network, and small part of your network goes over the Internet, and one thing here to understand is that you can only use AWS global accelerator when you terminate your VPN connection on the transit gateway.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:29: Only thing that you need to take care is that when you advertise the BGP parameters, for example, the local preference, the MED values, the AS path, the ASN numbers, they are exactly same if you want to enable the ECMP, right?
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:32: For example, the first half of the CIDR traffic will go through the first VPN connection and second half of the CIDR will go through the second connection, and on top of this, you can also advertise the BGP routes, which is aggregated route over both these VPN connections, so if any of the VPN connection is unhealthy or terminates, then also, your traffic is routed over the other active VPN connection, so this kind of traffic routing you can do to enable the ECMP along with the BGP routing, so what I want you to take from this is that with transit gateway and a VPN connection, you can have the ECMP, and you can have higher aggregated bandwidth.

- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:3: But there are certain caveats when it comes to attachments like VPN or a direct connect.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:12: So the user in that AWS account can perform only certain actions that you can define with these IAM permissions when you share the resources.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:17: Now, when you share transit gateway using RAM with other AWS account, then the other AWS account owner can connect the VPCs from that account with the transit gateway, which has been shared with that account.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:21: Further, if you're using a direct connect gateway to connect transit gateway to the direct connect, then you could also use direct connect gateway from other AWS account.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:23: Now, when a transit gateway shared with the AWS account, the other AWS account cannot really create, modify, delete the transit gateway route tables, and it cannot even enable or disability the route propagations and the associations.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:25: And finally, you need to make sure that when you choose the availability zone into the other VPCs which are going to connect to your transit gateway.

- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:34: So for example from the corporate data center, if you have to route traffic then this IP address will be used.

- 097_IPv4 and IPv6 traffic over AWS Site-to-Site VPN.txt:52: Now all these combinations are possible when you create the VPN tunnels and depending on that, IPv4 or IPv6 traffic is supported through these tunnels.

- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:7: So in general, when you create the site to site VPN connection, typically you will have your customer network somewhere at the distance place and then you will have your AWS region.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:16: And that's where many a times you may face challenges, for example having increased latency or there is congestion in the traffic, or there is some network disruption somewhere in any of the network.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:28: And that is because all these edge locations are connected to AWS region using AWS backbone network, which means when now traffic flows to the transit gateway over the VPN, first time it will hit the nearest edge location.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:64: So whenever you create the VPN tunnel, you have to use these two IPS for setting up that VPN connection and not the one which by default Transit Gateway will provide otherwise.

- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:34: So whenever customer gateway is behind the Nat, then you have to enable the Nat traversal and you have to open these ports.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:46: You know that when you enable the accelerated VPN using AWS Global Accelerator, you have to enable the Nat traversal on the customer gateway device.

- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:24: Now, when DPD occurs, that means when the DPD timeout occurs, there are three actions that you can set on AWS side of the VPN connection.

- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:37: And when this timeout occurs, in that case, you have to re-establish the VPN connection.

- 116_Direct Connect Connection Types - Dedicated vs Hosted.txt:13: Now, what does that mean is you can get the bandwidth in multiple of say 50, 100, 200, 300 or 500 Mbps, or in some cases when 1 Gbps, Gbps 5 and 10 Gbps as well.

- 126_Direct Connect with Transit VIF and TGW.txt:57: And we had seen similar pattern when we were connecting DX Gateway directly to the VPCs.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:7: Now, essentially what it means that if you have, say, for example, your own customer data centers and there is no AWS.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:10: For example, if it's a small company, then they might just go over the Internet for this connectivity and you know that Internet cannot provide you the consistent bandwidth.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:13: And then you are locked in for many years and many a times these are like point to point connections and you don't get much of the flexibility when you are to expand your network to other geographies.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:19: For example, if you are in the US, then you might want to select a Boston one.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:69: It supports both IPv4 and IPv6 routing prefixes and traffic, and it can support different kind of architectures like a full mesh architecture where you have multiple sites and you want to connect to those, or you want to make sure that your one site is connected to a particular site only and a third site cannot access that data.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:71: So imagine you have your data centers across the world, you are really a multinational company and you want to connect all these data centers together with AWS Backbone Network.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:76: The other type of connectivity you might want to have is maybe you have your own data centers and you want to connect to your partner networks and you could have multiple partners and you don't want that.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:86: For example, there is a production network and there is a test setup network and you don't want production, network and test network to go over the same connection.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:94: For example, you will have another set of connection whips connecting to different direct connect gateway to redirect that traffic to the prod workload only so you can very well set this kind of isolated connectivity as well.
- 127_Direct Connect SiteLink.txt:106: Just one thing you need to remember if you want to connect multiple customer data centers using AWS Backbone Network or a direct connect, then you can use AWS Sitelink.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:3: When the traffic goes out from AWS to on-premises network, you know that traffic starts from the VPC and then VPC itself has a route table.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:7: Typically, whenever you have multiple DX connections to reach out to different destinations.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:17: Now, when VPC has to make a decision where the traffic will go, then there are certain route priorities that AWS has defined.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:21: So for example, if there is a route for a particular IP address and it goes through, for example, internet Gateway, then even if there is another route for 10.10.2.0/24 and you have defined target for that as a VPC Peering connection, then the route through the internet Gateway will take precedence because you have defined it as the longest prefix which it matches with, then comes the static routes as compared to the propagated routes.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:60: Now, now DX Routing policies and BGP communities are used to influence the Routing decision when the traffic flows through the direct connect connections.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:79: So when you advertise your routes from on-premises to AWS side, you can restrict that your routes should be advertised to only base AWS Region or to that continent or they should be propagated globally in all AWS public network.
- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:85: And additionally, you can also use local BGP community tags to influence the traffic when there are multiple paths to reach to the target network on your side.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:4: So in this case, of course, when you create a public wifi, you remember you have to provide the IPv4 prefixes or IPv6 prefixes, right?
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:25: And once that is done, you advertise these routes and these routes get advertised to all AWS regions because public wifi allows you to connect to all AWS regions.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:33: Now in this case, how AWS takes that decision, like I want to take one path over the other, and this is essentially applicable when there are multiple direct connect connections between AWS and your on-premise networks, right?
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:60: We just talked about that when we say there is a No Export BGP community tag, which AWS sends along with the routes.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:64: So now with basic understanding of how the routing and BGP communities work, let's try to build some architecture when it comes to the traffic flowing between the AWS and on-premise network and when there are multiple direct connect connections.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:9: So considering the case, when you're using a public ASN for public VIF, then how the routing happens is that, on the customer gateway site, you have to advertise the same prefix with the same border gateway protocol.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:16: That means you can only use the private ASN for use cases like active-passive connection.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:28: Now when we say same BGP prefix as in, when you advertise your prefix information, then it should be same.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:50: Now, when AWS learns about this BGP parameters, it will take always the primary connection as, you know, the first choice, if the primary connection is done, then it will look for other paths.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:74: Now, this is a case when AWS wants to send the traffic to your router, right?
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:87: This is all when you are using the public ASN for your router.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:90: So for example, you will send something like a base address/25 here.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:102: Now for active-passive connection, things change a little when you use a public ASN or a private ASN.
- 130_Public VIF routing scenarios.txt:108: But then this is always good to know when you actually work on direct connect, and when you actually get a chance to set up those routes, right?
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:8: So typically when you receive the on-premises prefixes or routes or a BGP session on AWS site, you have a choice or you have a control where those routes can be propagated to all AWS regions or you know particular AWS region, for example, and the requirement could be, you only want to have the connection with one AWS region because your company do not operate or do not use other AWS regions so there is no point in opening the routes for the other regions to communicate with your on-premise network.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:16: So if I just want to show here, whenever routes are advertised from customer router to AWS customer route can set any of this BGP communities and when AWS sends the route of AWS public IPS and prefixes to the customer network, it can also set this BGP community tags.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:24: So for example, there is North America as a continent.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:56: Now, this is something like, when you receive the routes at your end, you can understand that these routes am receiving from a local AWS region in which I have Direct Connect connection.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:92: And there could be also routes sent by the public region like North Virginia, which is not part of the same content, in that case from Ireland, when the routes are received, it has the community tag as 72248200.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:95: So whenever routes are received along with whatever BGP community tag it has, along with that, additionally, there will be no export tags.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:101: So you want to only have the communication with local AWS region in that case, what you'll see, you'll see the routes coming in, say for example, routes from the Ireland region, and you will see that there is a BGP community tag 8200 but I don't want to have my communication with the Ireland region.
- 132_Private VIF routing policies and BGP communities.txt:3: So these policies and communities work differently, when it comes to the private VIF.
- 132_Private VIF routing policies and BGP communities.txt:46: Now, which one of that we can again control either VIF A or VIF B but understand that it uses the home or a local region as the first choice when the prefixes are advertised are the same length from multiple connections.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:11: Now there are few prerequisites or kind of considerations when you can create the lags.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:71: So when you enable the Macsec, it uses the Macsec secret key which is generated using the connection key name CDN and connectivity association key.
- 134_DX Connection Resiliency.txt:16: And again, it is not really recommended for critical workloads when you are really operating in a hybrid mode.
- 135_DX Failure detection with BFD.txt:2: Now that we have seen some of the architecture when it comes to the high availability of direct connect connections.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:5: And when we say private, that means there is actually a dedicated line as well from direct connect location to your data center.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:56: Now, just one more thing about this architecture is that you might be wondering that we don't have the direct gateway here in between, because you know that if you want to connect transit gateway to the direct connect connection, then you need to have the direct connect gateway.
- 136_DX Security & Encryption (VPN over DX and MACSec).txt:79: And just one more point about the MACSec is that when you configure your connection, you can specify one of the values for your MACSec and those values are, should encrypt, must encrypt, or no encrypt.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:4: And then we also saw when it comes to the VPC, what is an MTU that is supported when the traffic flows over to the internet or between VPCs over a VPC peering connection or through a VPN connection, right.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:5: Now, let's see how it is structured when it comes to the Direct Connect connection.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:13: So very first thing to remember, public VIFs don't support Jumbo Frames, and when it comes to the Transit VIF or Private a VIF, Transit VIF support the MTU of 1500 or 8500.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:16: Now this is supported when Private VIF is attached to the Virtual Private gateway or Direct Connect gateway.
- 137_DX support for MTU and Jumbo Frames.txt:17: And this is required that when you have to use Jumbo Frames on the VIFs of course the underlying Direct Connect connection or LAG should also be supporting the Jumbo Frames.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:2: Now, this is one of the most confusing topic, I would say, when it comes to the Direct Connect Billing.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:8: Okay, so when it comes to the Direct Connect, you know what parties are involved, there is AWS region, which is connected to on-premise data center via the Direct Connect location, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:12: Now in all this, what are the charges that you have to pay when you go for a Direct Connect.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:21: Now, when we say data charges out means, any data that is going from AWS to on-premise network, or to Direct Connect location, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:50: So for example, if source region is not Virginia, and the destination DX location is Las Vegas, then the data transfer OUT charges are $0.02 per GB.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:67: Now, you need to understand that when it's a dedicated connection, you request for a connection and then you get LOA CFA, that you submit it to your Direct Connect partner, or a Direct Connect location provider.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:82: Also, you need to understand that, the billing for port-hour stops when the dedicated connection or hosted connection is deleted from your AWS account, which means, if it is just in a downstate, does not mean that there is no charge, right?
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:104: So, this is a pretty simple when it is a private VIF, or a public VIF.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:117: So, this is one difference, when it comes to the transit gateway.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:119: If there are multiple AWS organization involved, for example, the Direct Connect connection is owned by AWS account, which is part of AWS organization one, and the resource which is sending the data, is a part of an AWS account, which is part of AWS organization two.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:152: So with that, I think, we saw lot of things when it comes to the DX billing.
- 138_DX Pricing.txt:153: And I think it is one of the, again, important topic to know, because then you're very clear, you know, when it comes to the questions related to the billing for DX So, thanks for watching.
- 139_DX Monitoring using CloudWatch.txt:13: So you are to make sure that you set CloudWatch alarm when your state becomes 0, probably you should know via the email or an SMS, whatever notification mechanism you want to implement.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:5: For example, there could be a issue at the physical layer or there could be issue at the routing layer.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:8: First thing, when there is no physical connectivity, that means you are not able to really receive any signals from the DirectConnect connection.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:11: When you go for a dedicated connection or a hosted connection, you have to make sure that the DirectConnect location provider has completed the Cross Connect.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:36: So for that, you are to make sure when you give the parameters for the BGP session, you give the correct ASN numbers.
- 140_DX Troubleshooting - Layer1-4.txt:43: But when you expose these configurations and apply the same key on your side of the router, that means customer side of the router, you don't make mistake while putting those keys.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:8: And in all likelihood, you would want to connect all these data centers to AWS regions and applications running inside those regions as well.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:13: So for example, you are storing data in S3, which is accessible over the Internet, and you want to make sure that all these services are accessible from your on prem data center as well as VPC should also be able to access your applications running in your on premise data center.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:18: Now examples could be if you are running high performance cloud that is HPC compute or you are working on some media kind of application like Netflix for example, you are uploading or downloading a lot of data or you are running some AV.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:35: You also want to connect your data centers to the transit gateways, and you already know that you can do that using the Transit Gateway and Direct Connect Gateway.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:56: We could have, but we are not, because Transit Gateway has a lot of charge when it comes to per GB data processing.
- 141_DX Architecture - Putting it together.txt:65: And finally, if you want to connect to the public services, like, for example, S3, again, you don't want to send that data through Transit Gateway or through your VPC because there would be additional cost.

- 142_DX Summary and Exam Essentials.txt:23: For example, all the connections should terminate on the same AWS Direct Connect endpoint, and the connection bandwidth should also be the same.
- 142_DX Summary and Exam Essentials.txt:52: Now, there are certain exceptions when you use transit gateways.
- 142_DX Summary and Exam Essentials.txt:53: And we have also talked about that use case.

- 142_DX Summary and Exam Essentials.txt:78: Now important parameters of course, are the for example the Vlan tags.

- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:6: For example, how do you connect multiple VPCs?
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:30: For example, there are two different AWS region in which you have your VPCs deployed.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:53: And on top of this, if you want to connect your direct connect connections, that means you are going now hybrid.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:65: So for example, a different network layer for development or production or shared services.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:103: For example, if you create a new attachment for the VPC, you can tag that attachment.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:120: So when you create a cloud Wan you first create a AWS cloud Wan global network, which is combination of core network and transit gateways network.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:126: Now, once you deploy your network, of course, you can then create the attachment to the core network, whether you have VPCs, you have SD-Wan, you have VPN, or you want to connect your existing transit gateway.
- 143_What is AWS Cloud WAN_.txt:151: For example isolated segments.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:56: When we talk about the segment sharing.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:64: Now for this, it is important to understand that whenever you create multiple segments on the core network, this segments are isolated, so all the attachments on those particular segments can communicate with each other, but the attachment across the segments cannot.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:68: For example, development segment can communicate with the shared services segment.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:85: For example, if there is segment A that you are defining and you are sharing segment A with segment B, C, and D through the segment action, then segment A will exchange route with B, C, and D, and also B, C and D will exchange the route with segment A, but this doesn't mean that B, C, and D will communicate with each other.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:87: When you define the policy for segment B and C kind of right.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:93: So these are all important parameters you need to consider when you create the segments.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:95: So these things will be much clear when you actually see that in action.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:120: So whenever you create any attachment it will see if there is a attachment policies which matches to that rule.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:127: For example, this attachment can only be connected to the segment if region is x, or this is for a particular VPC id, or this attachment has a particular tag key or tag value, or it is coming from particular AWS account.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:129: And when you do that, basically you can define that the method of associating your attachment to the segment is a constant or it's a tag based.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:130: So constant means that whenever you write the rule in that, you define that.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:131: I'm writing this rule for a particular segment, for example development segment.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:136: So whenever you create the attachment you will define the tag with say environment equal to development.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:141: And again we will talk about this behavior when we do our exercise.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:142: So these are all the important parameters when you create the core network policy.
- 144_Core Network Policy.txt:150: Now, when the policy gets into the ready to execute state, it also provides you change set, which means from version 1 to 2, what all changes you are making to the policy and how it will affect your network.

- 146_Connecting Transit Gateway & Direct Connect.txt:13: So one obvious choice is you don't use the transit gateway and rather reconfigure your VPCs to connect to your segment, which are there in your new cloud Wan network.

- 146_Connecting Transit Gateway & Direct Connect.txt:22: For example, you could have your production segment or your development segment.
- 146_Connecting Transit Gateway & Direct Connect.txt:24: And now in order to connect your transit gateway network to the core network, you have to map the Transit Gateway route tables to the corresponding segments of your cloud Wan network.
- 146_Connecting Transit Gateway & Direct Connect.txt:32: So in order to connect your direct connect you have to use the transit VIF Direct Connect Gateway.
- 147_AWS Cloud WAN Summary.txt:10: But whenever we talk about cloud Wan, typically we are referring to the core network connections.

- 333_VPC Peering.txt:11: And this is allowing you to connect them together.
- 333_VPC Peering.txt:19: So if you want to connect a VPC from one account A, to account B, you could, and also across regions.

- 339_Site to Site VPN, Virtual Private Gateway & Customer Gateway.txt:25: And then, when this is done, then the connectivity will work.
