- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:6: So what are the use cases for Direct Connect?
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:13: So we have a region and we want to connect it into our corporate data center.
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:21: The alternative is to connect to public services within AWS such as Amazon Glacier, Amazon S3.
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:24: So what if you want to connect to one or more VPCs in different regions?
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:26: So we have an example where we have two regions, and they have two different VPC, we have two different CIDRs and we want to connect our on-premise data center into both VPC.
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:27: So we're going to establish a Direct Connect connection, then using the private VIF, you're going to connect it to Direct Connect gateway.
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:41: Now, when you have a Direct Connect, there is no encryption.
- 341_Direct Connect & Direct Connect Gateway.txt:53: So in this use case, we have four connections across two locations, sorry, going into AWS.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:2: For example, you have many VPC and you want to peer them together, then you want to establish some VPN connections and direct connect, and then you have a direct connect gateway to connect to multiple VPC at a time, and this can become very, very complicated in terms of network topology.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:18: So, another use case for transit gateway is to increase the bandwidth of your site-to-site VPN connection using ECMP.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:22: And the use case, as I said, is to create multiple site-to-site VPN connections to increase the bandwidth of your connection to AWS using a site-to-site VPN.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:24: So when you establish a site-to-site VPN connection there are actually two tunnels, one going forward and one going back.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:25: When you are connecting such a VPN into a VPC directly, both of these tunnels are used as part of one connections, but when using the transit gateway two tunnels can be used at a time, so this is why you see two lines in this diagram.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:27: And so, when you have four tunnels of site-to-site VPN, you are increasing the throughput of your connection which is something you cannot do if you were to connect your corporate data center directly into a VPC.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:32: But also, you can add more site-to-site VPN connections into transit gateway, for example two or three, to double or triple your throughputs through ECMP.
- 343_Transit Gateway.txt:34: Obviously when you do the setup, you're going to have to pay for each GB of data going through the transit gateway, so there's an added cost to this performance optimization.

### 5. Constraints / Limitations
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:7: Then the only option you have is to set up some kind of private connectivity between these two VPCs.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:8: That means the traffic should not flow over the internet.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:19: Now, when AWS introduced VPC peering for the first time at that moment, it was possible only within the same AWS region.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:30: First thing is that the lead pieces that you connect should not be having the overlapping CIDRs.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:32: The address range of VPC should not overlap.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:34: So it should be non-overlapping.
- 054_VPC Peering.txt:35: That means if one VPC uses say 10.10.00/16, that another VPC cannot have the same address range.
- 055_Hands On_ VPC Peering across AWS regions.txt:3: So in this set of what we are going to do is, we should have two VPCs.
- 055_Hands On_ VPC Peering across AWS regions.txt:9: Now instance A is launched as a public subnet because we want to log in to that instance, and from there we should be able to test the connection to instance B.
- 055_Hands On_ VPC Peering across AWS regions.txt:10: But in ideal world maybe even this subnet could be private, and then you should have some other mechanism to get to this instance maybe you have some jump post and from there you can get into instance A, and from there you can get to instance B through peering connection.

- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:2: Now let's look at some of the limitations with VPC peering, and then some certain cases in which VPC peering connection won't work.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:4: So VPC peering should not have the overlapping CIDR between two VPCs.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:12: If you have a peer in connection from VPC A to B and from B to C, that does not make the traffic flowing through A to C, it cannot go like this and here, right?
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:13: So VPC A and VPC C still cannot communicate.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:15: Third, you cannot, you can set up only one VPC peering connection between any two VPCs.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:16: So between this A and B, there is only one peering connection.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:17: You might think, maybe for high availability or for more bandwidth, you have to set up multiple peering connection, but it is not required and you cannot even do that.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:20: So if more than 125 VPCs you want to connect to the, any of the VPCs, you cannot.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:28: That means the traffic cannot go from VPC A to the corporate data center.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:30: That means the traffic should end into the VPC B EC2 instance over network interface, only.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:31: It cannot go beyond that.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:33: Second, VPC peering connection cannot also get you to the internet gateway from another VPC.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:38: You can't have this connectivity.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:40: In VPC B If you have the NAT gateway, then also from VPC A through peering connection, you cannot reach to the internet via that NAT gateway.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:41: So understand that because then in the exam, you will know, given this situation, you cannot use VPC peering as an option here.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:45: So if you have VPC endpoint point created in VPC B, you can still reach to those end points and you can't access S3 or DynamoDb from VPC A through the VPC peering connection.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:47: So just to summarize, a VPC peering connection cannot be established If there are overlapping CIDR connection between VPCs, then VPC peering connections are non transitive.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:48: That means VPC connection from A to B and B to C does not mean you can go from A to C and then these are a few invalid scenarios where you can't reach to internet gateway or NAT gateway or VPC endpoint in the peered VPC from your original VPC.
- 056_VPC Peering invalid scenarios.txt:49: And these are important to understand for your exam.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:2: Now in my opinion, this is again one of the most important section because here we are going to talk all about the private connectivity from your VPC to other AWS services or the services which are hosted in VPC.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:5: And that's where it is one of the important topic.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:49: So these are the reasons for which you should use VPC endpoints.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:87: So the first VPC endpoint type is a gateway endpoint for which you have to update your route table of the subnet, and then all the traffic to the S3 or DynamoDB only for these two services, traffic goes privately from this instance to this AWS services.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:110: Now again, with respect to the types, there is a gateway endpoint which only enables the access to Amazon S3 and DynamoDB.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:119: And this is a small topic as compared to the interface endpoint for the reason that it only allows you to connect to the Amazon S3 and DynamoDB to only these two services.
- 057_Introduction to VPC Endpoint and PrivateLink.txt:122: There is no charge for using gateway endpoint, and that's what you should always use it in my opinion.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:17: But you must be wondering what kind of entry you will add, because for adding the route table entry, you need to know the IP addresses of Amazon S3 and DynamoDB.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:20: So ideally, you should add all those IP addresses to your route table, which is not really practical, right?
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:39: By default, you know that all the outbound traffic is allowed, but in case you are using a custom security group in which it is not allowed, then you should also open the port for https and http for the same prefix list.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:42: Further, one more important thing about VPC gateway endpoint is that they can be accessed from within the same VPC in which you have created it, and you can only access the AWS services in the same region.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:58: And that's why the in this case, you can't access this S3 bucket via this gateway endpoint.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:60: Similarly, if the traffic originate in on premise data center, it cannot flow through the gateway endpoint to S3.
- 058_VPC Gateway Endpoint.txt:65: And finally one more important thing about the gateway endpoint is that it's free to use.

- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:1: Okay, now that you understood VPC gateway endpoints, let's look at the more important part of the endpoints that is VPC endpoint and private link.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:14: While in this section we will be focusing on interface endpoints only.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:15: But just for your knowledge, you should also know that there are other types of the endpoints which use private link feature of aws VPC.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:26: The most important is interface endpoint.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:39: And this is really exciting because until now, the scope of the endpoint was regional, which means the VPC in which you create the endpoint should be in the same region as the service which you are accessing using the endpoint.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:42: Otherwise only TCP traffic was supported.
- 060_Introduction to VPC PrivateLink powered endpoints.txt:57: Some important features to know and some additional advanced concepts.

- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:6: Okay so let's get to this now important thing that you should know that when you create a VPC endpoints powered by Privatelink, it creates Eni into your subnet inside your VPC, right?
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:10: Another important thing to note is that because you have the local IP address, now you can connect to the services inside a VPC which has overlapping cidrs.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:18: And I will show you how the security group rule should be.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:25: So these are all the important points that you should know about VPC endpoint with private link.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:29: But in both the cases you should have non-overlapping ideas for those two VPCs.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:30: Otherwise you can't connect using the VPC peering connection or transit gateway.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:49: Now here we are not showing the availability zones, but in reality every VPC interface endpoint should be created in multiple availability zones.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:51: So ideally you should create VPC endpoint in these two subnets.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:61: Which means that you can also create the VPC endpoint only in one of the subnet.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:65: If there is some outage in the AZ, then you cannot access the target services because that is not functional.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:66: So for both the reasons high availability as well as to not to have the inter AZ data transfer charge, you should have multiple Enis across availability zones.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:73: And that's where there should be an inbound rule.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:74: Say for example, if you are accessing S3 over 403, then 443 traffic should be allowed from the client private IP address.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:78: So remember that interface endpoint any should have the inbound rules from the client okay.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:87: And the last important thing about the VPC interface endpoint is that it can be accessed from other networks.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:94: So that's a fundamental difference between VPC gateway endpoint where you can't access it from other networks, but VPC interface endpoints or in general VPC endpoints powered by private link can be accessed from the remote network.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:97: And with that again just putting this forward, these are all the important things that you should know about the VPC endpoint and private link.
- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:102: And it is really really important to understand so that it is very clear how to configure the VPC endpoints in real life.

- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:18: There are a number of connection limits, but here you can expose your services to thousands of consumer VPCs.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:23: And then you will have your actual services running on EC2 instances or on the containers, but they should be behind network load balancer.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:31: And now let's talk about a few important things that you should know about the VPC endpoint services.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:33: So important thing is you need to understand the AZ consideration.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:34: So because you are using the network load balancer in the service provider VPC, you know that you will typically create that across multiple availability zones because your targets were actually services are running should be in different AZS for high availability.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:35: Now if you do that when you create VPC endpoints, they should also be created in different AZS, right.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:43: So service provider VPC should have target across multiple AZS.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:45: Consumer VPC should also be created in corresponding AZS.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:46: In consumer VPC application should use zone specific DNS so that traffic remains in that AC only, and otherwise, if using the regional endpoint, then DNS will resolve to the Eni IPS of this VPC endpoint.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:50: And finally, to make this solution perfectly highly available for the network load balancer, you should also enable the cross zone load balancing so that if one of the AC is down, network load balancer can send traffic to the target in another AC.
- 064_VPC Endpoint Service.txt:52: But again, in this context you should also consider that.

- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:65: So this remote VPC cannot still resolve this public DNS to the VPC endpoint DNS.
- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:68: So for that what you should do is first you should disable the private DNS so that AWS does not create that private hosted zone.
- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:69: And which means that you should now create your own private hosted zone with the same name as S3 endpoint.
- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:70: And in that private hosted zone, you should create an alias record or a CNAME record pointing this S3 public DNS to the VPC endpoint DNS.
- 071_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from on-premises network.txt:10: And the connectivity should work.
- 071_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from on-premises network.txt:15: Now in this case, we can't follow the same approach as previous because in that case, we had associated the private hosted zone with VPC because VPC is a AWS construct, right?
- 071_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from on-premises network.txt:16: And as you know, this DNS cannot be directly resolved from the on premises network.
- 071_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from on-premises network.txt:22: So now as you have the route 53 inbound resolver, you should also configure the DNS forwarder on your side, which sends all the requests for S3 dot AP South one dot Amazon Aws.com to this route 53 resolver inbound endpoint IP address.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:32: But now let's look at the DNS resolution because that will be the most important thing to understand how it will work in this case.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:35: But the problem is this private hosted zone cannot be resolved from the, Though or remote or say, peered VPCs right now, if you have to do that, as we learned earlier, you have to first disable the private DNS for the endpoint.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:67: And what is important thing to understand or remember for your certification exam.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:5: But it is always important to understand when to use what right.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:9: So VPC peering is useful when there are many resources that should communicate between the peered VPC.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:12: Then you should use VPC peering connection, right?
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:15: Then you should use Privatelink, right?
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:16: So you should use it when you want to allow access to only a single application hosted inside your VPC to other VPCs without peering that VPCs.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:21: Further, when there is overlapping Cidrs, you know that you cannot connect them to the transit gateways or VPC peering.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:23: Further, there are also some limitations when it comes to the VPC peering connection.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:24: As in you can connect single VPC over, say 125 VPC peering connection only.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:29: So only service consumer can initiate the traffic and then return.
- 073_VPC Peering vs VPC Endpoints.txt:30: Traffic comes, but it is still unidirectional with respect to who can originate that traffic, which means service provider cannot initiate the traffic to the service consumer.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:7: Further, you should know that there is a VPC gateway endpoint and it enables the private connectivity to S3 or DynamoDB in the same AWS region.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:9: And remember that VPC gateway endpoint cannot be accessed over the Direct Connect or the VPN or VPC peering, right?
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:10: They can only be accessed from within the same VPC where you created the gateway endpoint.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:28: That means traffic can only be initiated from the consumer VPC.
- 074_Exam Essentials.txt:29: Further, with respect to the DNS, VPC interface endpoint receives the regional and zonal DNS name, and if you want to keep the traffic within the same AZ, then you should prefer to use the zonal DNS name right and otherwise for high availability, you can also use regional DNS name.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:2: Now Transit Gateway is one of the top three sections which are important for your AWS networking certification.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:4: Now, having said that, it doesn't mean that other sections are not important, but most of the questions in your exam will be from these three sections.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:5: So Transit Gateway is very important, and make sure that you understand everything that we discuss in this section.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:18: And VPC pairing doesn't provide the transitive routing, which means if VPCA is connected to B, and B is connected to C, then A cannot communicate with the VPC C.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:24: The point here is that you can't manage the network like this, right?
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:33: That means you can only attach the VPCs in the same region as Transit Gateways.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:40: Now understanding all these attachments is very important and we are going to dive deep into every type of the architecture in this section.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:7: That should be multiple VPCs and then there is transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:11: Now there are certain restrictions when you connect VPCs to the transit gateway and the obvious one is that VPC CIDR should not be overlapping.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:12: So you can't connect the VPCs which have the overlapping CIDRs.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:19: Now this route table governs how the traffic should flow across the attached networks.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:28: In this case the VPC CIDRs' routes are automatically propagated to the transit gateway, but then how does VPC know that the traffic which should go to say 10.1.0.0/16 should go through this VPC attachment, right?
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:32: So VPC should also have the route tables and those route table could be the VPCs default route table, that is the main route table, or you can have a subnet and the route table could be of that subnet.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:33: Now, if you see there is a route 10.0.0/8, and we are saying that the traffic should go through the transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:48: Now you can control basically how this route propagation happens, but as of now that is what is important to understand.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:49: And also remember that every VPC and the subnet route table also should have the entry and this entry has to be static.
- 077_Hands On_ Transit Gateway & VPCs with full routing.txt:6: Now, additionally, as I said, you also need to manage the route table on the subnet side of your VPC and in that you should at least have this static entry created.

- 077_Hands On_ Transit Gateway & VPCs with full routing.txt:12: Now, ideally we should just have the private subnet in all three VPCs and then all these VPCs would be connected via the transit gateway and the traffic can float across all the VPCs.

- 077_Hands On_ Transit Gateway & VPCs with full routing.txt:52: So in the security group, I have allowed the ICMP protocol, that means I should be able to ping from any IP from the CIDR range, 10.0.0.0/8.

- 077_Hands On_ Transit Gateway & VPCs with full routing.txt:81: Now this is important.
- 077_Hands On_ Transit Gateway & VPCs with full routing.txt:84: However, if you have the subnets which are in different AZ and you don't select that AZ at this moment, then transit gateway communication cannot happen with those subnets.

- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:7: So let's see how the design should look like while using the transit gateway.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:11: And when I say in each availability zone, it doesn't mean it's an ideal, but this is how it should be because transit gateway has a limitation where if you create the transit gateway attachment in a given AZ, then only the subnet from that AZ would be able to communicate with the transit gateway through that attachment.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:15: And that's where if you need connectivity across three availability zones, then you should have transit gateway attachment created in three availability zone in your VPC.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:21: So when you attach VPC to the transit gateway, you must enable one or more AZs to be used by transit gateway so that the traffic can be routed to the VPC subnet in those particular AZs.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:24: But remember that after you enable the AZ, traffic can be routed to all subnet in that AZ only.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:25: So resources that reside in the same AZ, where there is no transit gateway attachment cannot reach to the transit gateway, right?
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:26: So that's one important consideration when it comes to the transit gateway.
- 080_Transit Gateway AZ considerations.txt:27: Remember, in whichever AZ you are creating the transit gateway attachment, only the subnets from that AZ will be able to communicate to the transit gateway; other subnets will not be.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:2: Now, this is a small lecture, but this is an important topic, because we have seen this coming into the new exam.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:3: And this is really important to understand how transit gateway behaves with respect to the network flowing into the availability zones, right?
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:5: That means you can't send traffic from the subnet into the other availability zone if there is no ENI in that availability zone, right?
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:24: But that's not important here.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:43: Now, in case, if something fails, only one AZ is affected, right?
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:65: Now, at this moment, transit gateway has to make a decision to which ENI in this shared services VPC it should send traffic.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:68: And the route table of this subnet, in which this ENI is there, it will tell that traffic it should go to the appliance 2, which is here.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:80: That means it is monitoring for the incoming traffic and it will only allow the corresponding traffic to go back from the appliance if it matches the incoming traffic, right?
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:93: Now, what it makes transit gateway to do is to fix only one ENI for the flow, rather than looking at which AZ it is coming from.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:94: So if transit gateway knows that the traffic is coming from a particular IP address and particular port, and going to a particular IP address and a port, and with some protocol that's called flow hash algorithm, it will always, for the life of that flow, will send traffic to only that particular ENI, irrespective of which AZ the traffic has originated in.
- 081_Transit Gateway AZ affinity & Appliance mode.txt:113: This is important for the exam, because we have seen this kind of scenarios presented and you should know that appliance mode can solve this problem.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:4: You know that transit gateways are the regional routers, which means that if you have VPCs in one region, you can connect those VPCs to the transit gateway in the same region only, which means if you need a connectivity between multiple VPCs via the transit gateway, then what you could do is rather connect these transit gateways across the AWS region.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:11: Now, if you think to understand why you implement the transit gateway peering is that the routes that you would have to put on the transit gateway connection has to be static, and which means that you cannot have the BGP routing enabled for these two transit gateways.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:22: That means if you don't add this entry, then these two transit gateway cannot send those traffic.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:29: So if the scenario is given that the traffic across the region should be encrypted, then if you use any of the AWS private connectivity options like transit gateway peering or VPC peering, then the traffic is automatically encrypted, and as in bandwidth, it can support up to 50 Gbps bandwidth between these two transit gateways, so you get a fair enough bandwidth to have the high network throughput applications running across the two regions.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:30: And finally, you should use unique ASN.
- 082_Transit Gateway Peering.txt:31: Now, this is kind of best practice, but at this moment it doesn't matter, because a BGP is not supported between transit gateways via the peering connection, but in the future, AWS will enhance the capabilities, and at that moment, maybe you would be able to set up the BGP connections, and then those autonomous system numbers should not be the blocker there for you.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:8: And that's where it becomes important attachment to learn about.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:16: In order to connect this appliances to the Transit Gateway before creating this GRE tunnel, there should be a transport mechanism.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:23: In fact, for connect attachment, it is must to have the BGP peering.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:67: Only BGP support it for the Connect attachment.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:71: Now, if you compare this with the IPsec VPN, then IPsec VPN only provides 1.25 Gbps per connection.
- 083_Transit Gateway Connect Attachment.txt:78: And in that case, you should know that you can use Transit Gateway Connect attachment.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:15: There are 400-plus, but the way it optimizes your network traffic is that the traffic from on-premise network goes to the nearest edge location, and from there, it is already connected to AWS backbone network, so your path to the transit gateway mainly stays on the AWS network, and small part of your network goes over the Internet, and one thing here to understand is that you can only use AWS global accelerator when you terminate your VPN connection on the transit gateway.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:16: It is not supported if you are terminating your VPN connection on the virtual private gateway, so basically, the architecture that we had started this lecture with, so this is another important way in which you can optimize your network traffic while using site-to-site VPN with transit gateway.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:20: Now, that's for the reason that virtual private gateway device here has the limit of 1.25 Gbps aggregated bandwidth, and you can't increase that, and at the same time, you know that VPN tunnel also has 1.25 Gbps of maximum bandwidth limit, and, in general, if you want to load balance the traffic across multiple connections, then you need to have ECMP, that is equal cost multipath, which is like, you know, sending the traffic on both the links at the same time, and virtual private gateway doesn't support the ECMP.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:21: Now, for that reason, you can't have the higher bandwidth than 1.25 Gbps if you are terminating your VPN connection on the virtual private gateway.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:28: Now, one more thing to understand is that though you are getting more aggregated bandwidth, the per-flow bandwidth is still limited to 1.25 Gbps Now, per flow means per TCP connection, so if you are sending, say, 100-GB file over a single TCP connection, it will go through either of these four tunnels, and every tunnel is still limited to 1.25 Gbps bandwidth, so for that TCP connection, you will still be limited to 1.25 Gbps bandwidth, but if you are sending four files at the same time, then total bandwidth could be up to 5 Gbps, so I hope that is clear, so now, in this case, we are terminating both these VPN connections on the same customer router side, but you can also terminate these VPN connections on different customer router side.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:29: Only thing that you need to take care is that when you advertise the BGP parameters, for example, the local preference, the MED values, the AS path, the ASN numbers, they are exactly same if you want to enable the ECMP, right?
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:34: You can't use the static routing.
- 084_Transit Gateway VPN Attachment.txt:35: Okay, I hope that is clear, so as I said, we are going to talk more about the VPN and transit gateway in the VPN section as well, but in this lecture, I just wanted to touch upon these important concepts, and I hope that is clear.

- 087_TGW Architecture_ Centralized egress internet.txt:15: And for your networking certification exam, these are very important architectures because I'm sure there will be questions around such scenarios.
- 087_TGW Architecture_ Centralized egress internet.txt:18: Which means all these application VPCs when they want to reach to the internet, the traffic should flow through this shared services VPC.

- 087_TGW Architecture_ Centralized egress internet.txt:47: Now, with respect to the NAT gateway performance, because we are now centralizing all the egress through a NAT gateway, it shouldn't happen that NA gateway is a bottleneck.

- 087_TGW Architecture_ Centralized egress internet.txt:51: Now, these bandwidth limits might change in the future, but this is how it looks as of today.

- 087_TGW Architecture_ Centralized egress internet.txt:63: So rather, if you want to allow the inter-VPC communication, you should add the route table entries right here in this particular route table.

- 088_TGW Architecture_ Centralized traffic inspection with Gateway Load Balancer.txt:52: Now, if you see this appliance and Gateway Load Balancer endpoint route table, we have added one more route, which says if the traffic is going to internet, then it should go to the NAT gateway.
- 088_TGW Architecture_ Centralized traffic inspection with Gateway Load Balancer.txt:72: And more importantly, this is all about the Gateway Load Balancer because as I said, they are special.

- 090_Transit Gateway vs VPC Peering.txt:18: However, in case of transit gateway, there is a maximum limit of 50 gigabits per second per attachment referencing security group, which means if you have two VPCs and you are writing a security group rule for EC2 machine in one VPC, can you reference that security group in the security group of another VPC?
- 090_Transit Gateway vs VPC Peering.txt:32: So only the subnet, which are part of that particular AZ in which you are creating the attachment, will be able to route traffic through the transit gateway, right.

- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:12: So the user in that AWS account can perform only certain actions that you can define with these IAM permissions when you share the resources.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:14: And once that's done, all resources that you want to share will be shared with specified principle entities into AWS, and then those users or roles will only get access to the actions that you have mentioned in your permissions boundary.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:19: Now, a few caveats and important to know, while you use AWS Resource Access Manager to share the transit gateways, first, if you are using the site-to-site VPN attachment to the transit gateway, then you have to make sure that the VPN attachment is created in the same account as the transit gateway owner account.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:20: So you can't cross connect the VPN attachment across different AWS accounts.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:23: Now, when a transit gateway shared with the AWS account, the other AWS account cannot really create, modify, delete the transit gateway route tables, and it cannot even enable or disability the route propagations and the associations.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:28: Now for you or say for one account us-east-1A maybe a one availability zone, but in the same region, if there is another AWS account, then us-east-1A maybe a different availability zone, and probably AWS has done in that way because then otherwise everybody would keep launching the instances, maybe only first AZ, and that would make some kind of imbalance between resource capacity in those AZ.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:29: So, which means you shouldn't rely on the AZ name to identify a unique, AZ across different accounts, but rather AWS has assigned AZ IDs to the AZ and those remains constant irrespective of different AWS account.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:30: So instead of us-east-1A, you should look at the AZ ID and which could look like use1-az1, so us-east-1-az1.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:34: And this is important because you know transit gateway has AZ affinity.
- 091_Transit Gateway Sharing.txt:37: It's a small topic, but important to know that you can share transit gateways across AWS accounts and AWS organizations as well.

- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:1: Okay, now that you know how AWS Site to Site VPN works, let's look at some of the features and one of the feature of site to site VPN that is important to know is accelerated site to site VPN.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:17: And then your VPN connection might get terminated or it might not give the required bandwidth.

- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:51: Now another thing to remember is that you can only select this option while creating the VPN connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:52: Once you create that you can't change that later, right?
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:55: So going back to our discussion, a few important things that you need to know is that it is only supported on the transit gateway.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:56: That means if you are terminating your VPN on the virtual private gateways, then you can't have this VPN acceleration.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:57: Second, it can only be enabled while creating the VPN connection, which means that you can't enable that on the existing VPN connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:58: So in your exam, if there is a question which says that you want to accelerate the VPN, in that case, you can't really do that on existing connection.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:59: So the answer should be you should create a new connection and that too should terminate on AWS Transit Gateway.
- 099_Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN (Using AWS Global Accelerator).txt:69: Now another important thing for having the VPN acceleration you have to have Nat traversal enabled.

- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:2: The another important feature that you should know about AWS site to Site VPN is the Nat traversal, also written as Nat T.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:6: And important thing to note is that the customer gateway where we are terminating the VPN is publicly accessible.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:17: So in the regular case, if your customer gateway is publicly accessible, then you must allow inbound and outbound traffic for this two protocols and the ports UDP with 500 ports and ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:22: Now if this is the case basically Nat device cannot handle the ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:25: And that's where you cannot directly use the ESP 50 protocol here.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:40: So for Nat traversal, no configuration is required on AWS side, but on the customer gateway side.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:41: If you are not using Nat, then you must allow inbound and outbound for UDP 500 and ESP 50 protocol.
- 100_VPN NAT Traversal (NAT-T).txt:42: But if your customer gateway is behind the net, then you must allow inbound and outbound traffic on UDP port 500 and UDP port 4500.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:4: Now, in case of Static VPN, what happens, like you have to update the route tables on both sides of the connection, and you have to say like, if the traffic is going to particular destination on the right side, then the batch, the traffic should go through Virtual Private Gateway and similarly, route table you need to maintain on the Customer Gateway side, that if the destination is AWS CIDR, then the traffic should go through the Customer Gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:8: In that case on AWS side, you need to update your route table for your subnets of course and tell that if the traffic is going for that network C, then also it should take the path through VPN gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:13: Now, one thing to note, there is that AWS side of the route tables, that means the route table, which are attached to subnets, cannot have more than a hundred propagated routes.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:14: Now that's a limitation on AWS side, which means if on the customer side, if you are sending more than hundred routes through BGP, your connection might break, and we don't know how the exact behavior it would be, but the chances are your VPN connection can get terminated because AWS side of the network cannot tolerate or cannot accept more than hundred propagated routes.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:23: It says if the traffic is going to 10.10.0016 which is a VPC CIDR, the traffic should go through the Customer Gateway.
- 101_VPN Route Propagation (Static vs Dynamic).txt:26: So remember that on AWS side, you can only accept a hundred routes from the customer network side.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:16: But in this case, VGW is not ENI, it's a gateway and that's where it can't route the traffic directly from VGW to IGW.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:18: That means you cannot reach to the internet via the VGW, remember this.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:19: Now, moving on, what if now, instead of we said that you cannot go directly to the IGW, but what if we introduced NAT gateway in between?
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:22: And due to NAT gateway limitations, we cannot still reach to the internet if the traffic lands to VGW, and then from there, we can't really go to the internet via NAT gateway.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:27: So remember, for sending the traffic to internet via IGW, you can't use internet gateway directly or NAT gateway directly, but you can have your NAT EC2 instance and then that traffic can flow.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:32: That means if traffic is landing into this VPC from outside, this traffic cannot flow to the other peered VPC directly, unless you are originating that traffic here in the primary VPC.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:42: This traffic cannot flow through virtual private gateway.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:44: We said this is an invalid scenario, where traffic is coming from on-premise through virtual private gateway, or say VPN connection, you cannot reach to AWS services through a gateway endpoint.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:48: Now, if you see, I have some destination in my VPC where the traffic can land, and from there through that ENI, I should be able to go to the respective services.
- 102_VPN Transitive Routing scenarios.txt:65: So if traffic is originating from your on-premises and going to AWS via virtual private gateway, then you cannot access internet through a VPC attached internet gateway, you cannot access internet through a NAT gateway, you cannot access peered VPC resources through peering connection, and you cannot access S3 and DynamoDB via the VPC gateway endpoint.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:3: Now, you know that AWS site-to-site VPN provisions two tunnels, that's for high availability and it's up to you how you configure this tunnels, weather in active-active mode, which means both the tunnels are up at the same time and you can also configure it in active-passive mode, that means only one tunnel is up at any moment of the time.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:18: Now, if your traffic originates on AWS side of the network, for example, this EC2 instance here wants to send some traffic to any of those servers in corporate data center, it will send the packet to virtual private gateway and then this VGW will randomly select one of the tunnel, either tunnel 1 or tunnel two, you can't really influence that decision in case of static routing.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:22: Now what that means is, only one of the tunnel up at any moment of the time.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:23: So tunnel two is for example down, if you remember our hands-on exercise, we had set up opens one tunnel and we had configured only one tunnel at that time, we did not configure another tunnel.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:25: Now in this case, let's walk through the same scenario again, from your corporate data center if traffic goes to AWS first, it will go to customer gateway from there only one tunnel is up, there is no choice and you have to get to the VGW, VGW will send traffic to EC2 and from there return traffic will come to VGW, now VGW will see that there is only tunnel up, so there is no choice to go through another tunnel of course it will send traffic back from the same tunnel through which the traffic came.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:26: So in that case, you don't really need to worry about asymmetric routing because there is only one active tunnel.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:32: AWS does not have to do anything there, this is completely in your control, which tunnel should be up at any moment.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:37: So, this time, we have active-active tunnels and it would make sense to have only active-active tunnels because then there is no purpose of having two tunnels for high availability, right?
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:51: Now this is only possible in case of dynamic routing because you are having to BGP peers, one at VGW and other at CGW end.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:56: So this is very interesting and very important to know for you, may not be directly asked in a exam, but maybe in your day to day work you can analyze why you have asymmetric routing and how to fix that.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:59: In case of static routing with active-passive tunnel mode, that means only one tunnel is up at the moment, you don't have to really worry because always that tunnel will be used for both sides of the communication.
- 103_VPN Tunnels - Active_Passive Mode.txt:61: Now in case of dynamic routing, of course it will make sense to have active-active tunnel mode only because, active-passive, you are just not leveraging the high availability of the tunnels.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:3: And this is important because many times probably you want to fail-over to another channel or another network for your VPN connection or for sometimes, maybe you want to close that connection due to security reasons.

- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:29: Customer, which uses the Dynamic Routing must support the DPD at their router.
- 104_VPN Dead Peer Detection (DPD).txt:46: So that's it about DPD important for your exam to have a fail-over scenarios.

- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:10: Second, at Layer2, you need to have 802.1Q VLAN encapsulation protocol enabled and we'll talk about that third, auto negotiation of the ports must be disabled.

- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:22: And for DirectConnect, you must support this protocol.

- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:49: And for the connections which are 1 Gbps or less, it depends on the router vendor, whether this setting should be enabled or disabled.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:52: So DX supports BGP and at your side of the router should also support the BGP protocol.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:54: So please revisit if required.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:64: So those are the technical requirements for DX connection just to reiterate again, you should have single mode fiber.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:66: You should have Layer2 protocol that is 802.1 Q VLAN encapsulation.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:67: The auto negotiation for the ports must be disabled for the connection greater than 1 Gbps.
- 114_Direct Connect network requirements.txt:68: And then most important is that your router should support the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) with MD5 authentication.

- 128_DX Routing policies and BGP communities.txt:78: And with respect to the public VIF, you can't use to influence the path selection, but you can use it to influence how far your route should be propagated in the other network.

- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:11: Now traffic must be destined to AWS public prefixes.

- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:16: It makes sure that AWS is receiving traffic only from those prefixes.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:24: So first thing you must own these networks and AWS will verify that before setting up this BGP connection.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:47: And this is important because otherwise, if your network does not know about those public IP prefixes, then it does not really help you to route the traffic to AWS.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:49: Now what that means is once you learn the routes from the AWS, you should not further export these routes to the other networks outside of your network.
- 129_Public VIF Routing policies.txt:53: You should not further export these routes to the other networks.

- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:30: If you think that you operate or use only North America based US regions, then you will set the BGP community tag is 9200.
- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:35: Now, if you are using the community tag as 92249100, then all the routes received are only published or advertised to the local AWS region in which the Direct Connect connection is located.

- 131_Public VIF BGP Communities.txt:97: Now, what you can do is you will receive this routes as well as BGP communities at your end and with the NO_EXPORT, you can't really have this advertised to other route table or a public route table which are all over the internet, right?

- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:15: Which means if you have hosted DCS connection then you cannot create the lag on that.
- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:16: Now it can aggregate up to maximum four connections only and those connections will be in active mode.

- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:22: So this is an important consideration while creating the lag.

- 133_DX Link Aggregation Groups (LAGs).txt:67: Okay, just one more thing that you should know for the lag is that you can enable the macsec with the lag.

- 333_VPC Peering.txt:7: So in order for them not to connect, remember, I said, the VPC network CIDRs must be distant from each other because when we connect them together, if they have overlapping CIDRs, they will not be able to communicate.
- 333_VPC Peering.txt:9: That means that each VPC that wants to communicate with one another must have VPC peering enabled.
- 333_VPC Peering.txt:14: This is very, very, very, very important.
- 333_VPC Peering.txt:15: Finally, even though your VPC are peered with each other, you must also make sure that you update all the route tables in each VPC's subnets, to ensure that these two instances in different VPC, can communicate with each other.
- 335_VPC Endpoints.txt:19: And so for this, we must attach a security group because there is an ENI and it supports most AWS services if not all, and there's a cost to it, it's a cost per hour and it's a cost per gigabyte of data processed.
- 335_VPC Endpoints.txt:25: And there are only two targets for a Gateway Endpoint, it is Amazon S3 and DynamoDB, but the advantage that this is free and this scales automatically because it's only a route table access.
- 335_VPC Endpoints.txt:26: So, it's only for Amazon S3 and DynamoDB, remember this.
- 335_VPC Endpoints.txt:28: So the question you may ask me is, hey, if the Interface Endpoint supports everything and the Gateway Endpoint supports Amazon S3 and DynamoDB, should I use the Gateway or the Interface Endpoint for Amazon S3 or DynamoDB?

### 6. Patterns / Architectures

- 061_VPC PrivateLink - Important to know features.txt:28: So without endpoints what you will typically do you will connect this to VPCs or say VPC peering connection or transit gateway.

- 068_Other VPC endpoint types - Gateway Load Balancer, Resource, Service-network.txt:68: So whether it's a peered VPC through the transit gateway or maybe a client sitting on premises and connected over VPN or DX.
- 069_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing on-premises services.txt:4: If you are using, say, too much of VPC peering connections or too much of transit gateways.
- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:24: So as long as this remote VPC is connected to this VPC over VPC peering connection or the transit gateway, it can reach to this IP address and can access the target service.
- 070_VPC Endpoint architectures - Accessing from peered or connected VPC.txt:31: Now if this client has to access S3 service privately through this VPC peering connection or transit gateway, and then through the interface endpoint, how that traffic will look like.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:16: So if you centralize the VPC endpoints here in the central VPC, and if you associate this VPC with the other VPCs over VPC peering connections or the transit gateway, then using just these three VPC endpoints, you can grant access to all these services to all other VPCs.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:26: But you can very well use Transit Gateway as well.

- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:37: And then you have to associate this private hosted zone with all the spoke VPCs.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:42: So traffic will flow from your spoke VPCs to ultimately the endpoint services.
- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:47: So here we used the private hosted zone and we associated that with all the spoke VPCs.

- 072_VPC Endpoint architectures - Centralized VPC endpoints.txt:59: Now, if any host inside this spoke VPCs try to resort to this DNS, we'll send all this DNS queries to this inbound resolver in the centralized VPC.

- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:1: Hello and welcome to this section, AWS Transit Gateway.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:2: Now Transit Gateway is one of the top three sections which are important for your AWS networking certification.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:5: So Transit Gateway is very important, and make sure that you understand everything that we discuss in this section.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:6: And one more thing that I want to mention at this point is that it is difficult to just understand the Transit Gateway and all its features without understanding the Direct Connect and the site-to-site VPN, because in reality all these three components typically work together to build some architecture.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:7: So in this section, I have tried my best to keep the discussion focused on the Transit Gateways, but ultimately you will be able to connect all these dots after you complete your other section, and specifically the VPN and the Direct Connect section.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:11: Okay, so what is Transit Gateway?
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:12: Now, I hope you already know at this point what is Transit Gateway and why they were introduced.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:13: But just to give you a brief history, Transit Gateways were introduced in 2018, and intent was to simplify the AWS networking when it comes to managing the multiple VPCs, and when you have to have the hybrid connectivity between AWS and on-premises network.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:14: So one of the key benefits of using Transit Gateway is to simplify the inter VPC network.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:17: Now without Transit Gateway, you know that you would have to do VPC peering connection and VPC peering connection is one to one, which means you have to attach two VPCs to each other.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:18: And VPC pairing doesn't provide the transitive routing, which means if VPCA is connected to B, and B is connected to C, then A cannot communicate with the VPC C.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:26: And that's where AWS introduced Transit Gateway, which greatly simplifies this architecture to as simple as this.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:27: So you connect all your VPCs to the Transit Gateway and then Transit Gateway can enable the inter VPC communication.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:28: Now in this entire section, we are going to cover a lot about the Transit Gateway.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:30: Now, Transit Gateway supports different kinds of attachments.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:31: So we just saw the VPC attachment, but Transit Gateway also supports peering connection between the Transit Gateways.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:32: Now this is because Transit Gateways are regional routers.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:33: That means you can only attach the VPCs in the same region as Transit Gateways.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:34: Now if you want to enable the communication between VPCs across different region, then rather you can create Transit Gateway in the respective regions, and then you can peer the Transit Gateway in the same way you peer the VPCs across different regions.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:37: That is a third attachment type, which is extending your SD-WAN network and connect it AWS network using the Transit Gateway.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:41: Now, apart from that, in this section, we are going to see a lot of features of Transit Gateway, like how it supports the multi-cast routing, what's the maximum transmission unit size of the Transit Gateways, how you use Appliance mode, AZ consideration, and how can you share the Transit Gateway across different AWS accounts.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:43: So Transit Gateway enables you to have the centralized resources to be used across multiple VPCs.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:44: So for example, you have a lot of spoke VPCs and you want to allow the internet connection through the Transit Gateway, through the centralized VPC.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:46: Now all those kind of scenarios can be implemented using the Transit Gateway centralized architecture.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:49: And also how can you reach to other AWS services using VPC endpoint, which are also centralized through the Transit Gateways.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:54: So this is a Transit Gateway peering across the region, and then this is how you connect Transit Gateway to the on-premise network over the VPN attachment.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:55: And similarly, you can have Transit Gateway connecting to your Direct Connect connection or the Direct Connect Gateway.
- 075_Introduction to Transit Gateway.txt:57: So ultimately, Transit Gateway can connect to all these attachments, and you can also have centralized traffic inspection by putting your virtual appliances into the centralized VPC and enabling the traffic inspection for north, south, or east, west traffic.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:2: Now that we have seen that transit gateway can be used to connect VPCs, VPN connection, direct connect gateway, as well as you can have the third party softwares, and transit gateways can be peered across the regions as well.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:3: Now, in this lecture, let's look at how to connect VPCs to transit gateway with VPC attachments.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:5: So now let's look at how VPCs connect to the transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:7: That should be multiple VPCs and then there is transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:8: And what you do is create an attachment with the transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:10: Now, in this architecture I'm showing you three attachments and there is a centralized transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:11: Now there are certain restrictions when you connect VPCs to the transit gateway and the obvious one is that VPC CIDR should not be overlapping.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:14: Now, as I said, with transit gateway, you get full mesh kind of connectivity, right?
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:17: Now let's take an example there is VPC A, B, and C with non-overlapping CIDRs as shown here, and then you create centralized transit gateway, and then you create three attachment, attachment A, attachment B, and attachment C.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:18: Now when you create a transit gateway, there will be a default route table of that transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:21: Now as soon as you attach your transit gateway to the VPCs, the VPC CIDR route get propagated to this transit gateway route tables.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:22: So all the routes that means 10.0.0.0/16, and 10.1.0.0/16 and 10.2.0.0/16 will automatically get propagated to the transit gateway route table, something like this.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:23: Now, as it happens, now if the traffic from VPC A reaches to the transit gateway, it will look at the default route table and it will see how to reach to the other VPCs, right?
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:25: So that's what transit gateway default route table does.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:26: Now at the same time, you also need to make sure your VPCs and the subnet also have corresponding route tables and those route tables have a route to reach to the transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:28: In this case the VPC CIDRs' routes are automatically propagated to the transit gateway, but then how does VPC know that the traffic which should go to say 10.1.0.0/16 should go through this VPC attachment, right?
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:31: So the route has to be managed at both end, the transit gateway side as well as the VPC side.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:33: Now, if you see there is a route 10.0.0/8, and we are saying that the traffic should go through the transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:36: It doesn't automatically understand that the transit gateway supports the routing to all these CIDRs, and that will be propagated to this VPC route table, it doesn't happen like that.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:41: It says the traffic is part of 10.0.0/8 so it will take the transit gateway route.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:42: As soon as it reaches the transit gateway, it will follow the transit gateway route table, and it will check is there a route to reach to 10.1.0/15?
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:45: Now this is how the routing happens when you use transit gateway.
- 076_Transit Gateway VPC attachments and Routing.txt:46: Now, thing to remember is that transit gateway has a default route table.
